heroOverview: Geotrichosis is a rare fungal infection caused by Geotrichum candidum, a yeast-like organism found in soil, dairy products, and the normal human gut and skin microbiome. Because it primarily affects people with weakened immune systems — including those with HIV/AIDS, haematological cancers, or prolonged corticosteroid use — it sits in the category of opportunistic mycoses that most healthy individuals never encounter. The lungs, mouth, and gastrointestinal tract are the most commonly infected sites, though disseminated disease involving the blood and multiple organs occurs when immune defences are severely compromised. Diagnosis relies on demonstrating the characteristic barrel-shaped arthroconidia in cultures or microscopy of sputum, stool, or tissue samples. Treatment with antifungals such as itraconazole or amphotericin B can be effective, but outcomes depend heavily on the degree of immunosuppression and the speed of recognition.

keyStats.prevalence: Rare; estimated to account for fewer than 1 in 100,000 fungal infections annually, with most cases reported in immunocompromised patients
keyStats.demographics: Primarily affects adults with haematological malignancy, HIV/AIDS, or organ transplantation; mild carriage found in up to 30% of healthy adults in the gut
keyStats.avgAge: No dominant age group; peaks in adults receiving immunosuppressive therapy
keyStats.globalCases: Fewer than 200 well-documented case reports in the modern literature, though carriage of Geotrichum is widespread worldwide

definition: Geotrichosis (ICD-10: B48.3) is an infection caused by Geotrichum candidum and its teleomorph Galactomyces geotrichum — a dimorphic fungus classified among the opportunistic ascomycetes. Under the microscope, Geotrichum forms chains of rectangular barrel-shaped arthroconidia that break apart from hyphal segments, distinguishing it from other common moulds. The organism colonises healthy people without causing harm; pathogenic infection emerges when host immunity falters. Disease may be localised to the bronchopulmonary tree, the oral cavity (thrush-like plaques), the digestive tract, or the skin, and the most dangerous form is disseminated geotrichosis in which the bloodstream carries the fungus to multiple organs simultaneously. The condition falls within the specialist scope of infectious disease and, in haematology oncology settings, often involves haematologists as well. Because Geotrichum is part of the normal microbiota, colonisation and true infection must be distinguished carefully: clinical significance is established only when the organism is isolated repeatedly from symptomatic sites or from a sterile body fluid.

typesClassification.description: The most common clinically significant form, producing chronic cough, mucopurulent sputum, haemoptysis, and radiological infiltrates resembling tuberculosis or aspergillosis; most often reported in patients with underlying lung disease or immunosuppression.

White plaques on the tongue and buccal mucosa that mimic candidiasis, occurring in patients with debilitating illness; distinguished microscopically by the barrel-shaped arthroconidia rather than pseudohyphae of Candida.
Diarrhoea, cramping, and mucosal ulceration in the stomach or intestines, described mainly in malnourished or immunocompromised patients; Geotrichum is isolated from stool.

The most severe form: fungaemia with spread to the liver, spleen, kidneys, skin, or brain; carrying the highest mortality and almost exclusively occurring in profoundly neutropenic or late-stage HIV patients.
Rare superficial skin lesions, sometimes seen as a secondary manifestation of disseminated disease, presenting as papules or pustules especially on the face and trunk.

primarySymptoms: Persistent productive cough with mucopurulent or blood-tinged sputum in the bronchopulmonary form, often mistaken initially for bacterial bronchitis or tuberculosis.
White, creamy plaques or patches on the tongue and inner cheeks that can be wiped away, resembling oral candidiasis, seen in the oropharyngeal form.
Chronic diarrhoea, loose stools, and lower abdominal cramping reflecting gastrointestinal mucosal involvement.
Low-grade fever and night sweats, particularly in patients with concurrent immunosuppression or underlying malignancy.
Fatigue and unintentional weight loss in prolonged or disseminated infection.
Shortness of breath on exertion or at rest when pulmonary infiltrates are extensive.
Haemoptysis — coughing up small amounts of blood — arising from erosion of airway mucosa by the fungal infection.
Skin papules or pustules, most often on the face, in patients with disseminated disease.
Abdominal pain and nausea from gastrointestinal involvement or hepatic spread.
Confusion and altered mental status in the rare cases where the central nervous system is involved.
earlyWarningSigns: A new productive cough in an immunocompromised patient that does not clear with standard antibacterials.
Unexplained white oral patches appearing in a patient on corticosteroids or chemotherapy.
Recurring loose stools without an identified bacterial pathogen in an immunosuppressed individual.
A persistent low-grade fever with no obvious source in a patient with haematological malignancy.
emergencySigns: High fever with rigors and haemodynamic instability (low blood pressure, rapid heart rate) suggesting fungaemia and septic shock.
Rapid worsening of respiratory function with oxygen saturation dropping below 92% in a patient already on antifungals.
Sudden neurological deterioration — new confusion, focal weakness, or seizures — indicating possible CNS spread.
Profuse haemoptysis requiring immediate airway protection.
Neutropenic fever in a patient with haematological cancer: any febrile episode in someone with neutrophil count below 500 cells/µL is a medical emergency.

causes.description: Geotrichum candidum colonises the gastrointestinal tract and skin of up to 30% of healthy individuals without causing disease. When immune surveillance falls — through chemotherapy-induced neutropenia, HIV-mediated CD4 depletion, or prolonged steroid use — the colonising organisms breach mucosal barriers and establish invasive infection, the same mechanism seen with Candida.

Geotrichum is abundant in soil, rotting vegetation, and unpasteurised dairy; inhaling airborne arthroconidia can initiate primary pulmonary infection in susceptible hosts, producing radiological infiltrates and productive cough that progress if the immune defect is not corrected.

causes.cause: Disruption of the normal gut barrier
causes.description: Chemotherapy-induced mucositis, inflammatory bowel disease flares, or prolonged antibiotic courses that eradicate competing bacterial flora allow Geotrichum to multiply in the gut and translocate across the damaged mucosa into the portal circulation, seeding disseminated infection.

Corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, and anti-rejection drugs used after organ transplantation impair neutrophil and macrophage killing of fungal cells, turning a normally harmless commensal into a pathogen and explaining why transplant recipients account for a disproportionate share of reported cases.

Neutrophils are the primary defence against fungal hyphae. Leukaemia, lymphoma, or aplastic anaemia producing neutrophil counts below 500 cells/µL for more than 10 days creates the highest-risk window for invasive Geotrichum infection, similar to the risk window for invasive aspergillosis.

riskFactors.description: CD4 counts below 200 cells/µL impair T-cell-mediated killing; Geotrichum is one of several opportunistic fungi that can exploit this defect, placing HIV-positive patients at measurably higher risk than the general population.

Patients with leukaemia, lymphoma, or myelodysplastic syndrome undergoing induction chemotherapy experience prolonged neutropenia, the strongest single risk factor for invasive Geotrichum infection in case series.
High-dose or prolonged steroids suppress both neutrophil function and T-cell immunity; using the lowest effective dose and tapering as quickly as clinically safe can reduce this risk.

riskFactors.factor: Solid organ or bone marrow transplantation
riskFactors.description: Anti-rejection immunosuppression raises infection risk; antifungal prophylaxis protocols in transplant centres are partly designed to address opportunistic mycoses including rare ones like geotrichosis.
Antibiotics disrupt the normal bacterial microbiota that competitively inhibits fungi; removing this competition allows Geotrichum to expand from a commensal population into a pathogenic load.
Protein-energy malnutrition impairs innate and adaptive immunity; historically many reported cases of gastrointestinal geotrichosis came from malnourished patients in resource-limited settings.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational or dietary exposure to Geotrichum
riskFactors.description: Farm workers, dairy handlers, and people who regularly consume unpasteurised cheese or milk face higher environmental inoculum, a theoretical contributor to primary infection when immunity is borderline.

affectedDemographics: Adults with HIV/AIDS and CD4 counts below 200 cells/µL
Patients undergoing induction chemotherapy for leukaemia or lymphoma
Solid-organ and haematopoietic stem-cell transplant recipients on immunosuppression
Patients receiving prolonged high-dose systemic corticosteroids
Malnourished individuals, particularly in low-income regions
Elderly patients with multiple comorbidities and impaired innate immunity
diagnosisOverview: Geotrichosis is diagnosed by combining clinical suspicion with laboratory confirmation, because the organism's presence alone does not prove pathogenic infection. In a patient with an appropriate risk profile and compatible symptoms — productive cough with pulmonary infiltrates, oral plaques, or prolonged diarrhoea — cultures of sputum, stool, or tissue are submitted to a mycology laboratory. Geotrichum grows on standard fungal culture media such as Sabouraud dextrose agar at 25-37°C within 2-5 days, producing white, powdery colonies. The key morphological hallmark is chains of rectangular barrel-shaped arthroconidia on wet mount; this appearance, combined with biochemical tests or matrix-assisted laser desorption ionisation time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF), confirms the species. Blood cultures are obtained when disseminated disease is suspected, though fungaemia is inconsistently detected. Chest CT in pulmonary disease may show consolidation, nodules, or cavities. Because Geotrichum can be a contaminant or commensal, clinical significance requires repeated positive cultures from symptomatic sites or a single positive culture from a normally sterile site such as blood or cerebrospinal fluid. Histopathology of biopsy material showing tissue invasion confirms infection definitively. Beta-D-glucan, a broad fungal marker, may be elevated but is non-specific.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Isolates Geotrichum candidum from an infected site, enabling species identification and antifungal susceptibility testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You provide a sputum sample by deep coughing, a stool sample, or a clinician takes a tissue biopsy or bronchoalveolar lavage; the sample is plated on fungal media and results are available in 2-7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Wet-mount microscopy with lactophenol cotton blue
diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises the characteristic barrel-shaped arthroconidia, providing a rapid presumptive diagnosis within hours.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The laboratory mounts a small portion of the culture or clinical sample on a glass slide and examines it under the microscope; no specific patient preparation is needed beyond providing the specimen.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapidly identifies Geotrichum species from culture isolates within minutes, improving on traditional biochemical identification.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in the laboratory on the fungal colony; from the patient's perspective, only the initial specimen collection is required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects fungaemia in suspected disseminated geotrichosis, confirming bloodstream involvement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two to three sets of blood are drawn from different venous sites into specialised culture bottles; results may take 24-72 hours or longer for fungi.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises the pattern and extent of pulmonary involvement, distinguishing geotrichosis from tuberculosis, aspergillosis, and other infiltrative lung diseases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in a scanner for about 10-15 minutes; contrast dye may be injected via a hand or arm vein to better delineate lesions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A non-specific marker of invasive fungal infection that, when elevated, supports the diagnosis and can guide treatment in the right clinical context.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results are usually available within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms invasive infection by demonstrating Geotrichum arthroconidia within tissue and is the definitive method for distinguishing colonisation from disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small sample is taken under local or general anaesthesia depending on the site, processed, stained with Gomori methenamine silver or PAS, and examined by a pathologist.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of geotrichosis is guided by the site and severity of infection and the degree of host immunosuppression, always combined with efforts to reverse or reduce the underlying immune defect where possible. For localised pulmonary or oral disease in patients with mild immunosuppression, oral itraconazole (200-400 mg daily) is the most commonly used agent and typically achieves clinical improvement within 4-8 weeks. Voriconazole, which has broader mould coverage, is an alternative backed by case-report experience. For disseminated or life-threatening geotrichosis, intravenous amphotericin B remains the anchor drug, particularly its lipid formulations that allow higher doses with reduced nephrotoxicity; duration is usually 2-6 weeks or longer depending on clinical response. Fluconazole is generally less effective because Geotrichum exhibits intrinsic reduced susceptibility to this azole. Antifungal susceptibility testing should be performed whenever an isolate is obtained, since in vitro minimum inhibitory concentrations correlate with outcome in invasive infections. Reducing immunosuppression — tapering corticosteroids, initiating or optimising antiretroviral therapy in HIV patients, or allowing neutrophil recovery after chemotherapy — is as important as the antifungal drug itself. Antifungal treatment may need to continue until immune reconstitution is achieved. Surgical drainage or debridement is occasionally required for focal abscesses or necrotic pulmonary lesions that fail to respond to medical therapy alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole
medicalTreatments.description: An oral triazole antifungal active against Geotrichum candidum; given at 200-400 mg daily, it is the preferred first-line agent for localised pulmonary, oral, or gastrointestinal geotrichosis. Blood-level monitoring is advised because absorption is variable and depends on gastric acid.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series report clinical response rates of approximately 60-80% for localised geotrichosis when itraconazole is combined with correction of the underlying immune defect.

medicalTreatments.name: Amphotericin B (lipid formulation)
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous polyene antifungal with broad-spectrum fungicidal activity; used for disseminated or severe geotrichosis. The liposomal formulation (3-5 mg/kg/day) is preferred over conventional amphotericin B because it causes significantly less nephrotoxicity at the doses needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates vary widely with severity; in disseminated disease with persistent neutropenia, mortality remains approximately 40-60% even with treatment, reflecting the underlying host status rather than drug failure alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Voriconazole
medicalTreatments.description: A second-generation triazole with potent anti-mould activity used as an alternative to itraconazole, particularly when tolerability or drug interactions preclude itraconazole; given as oral 200 mg twice daily or intravenous loading doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Published case reports document clinical cure in approximately 70% of treated patients, though controlled trial data are absent given the rarity of the condition.

medicalTreatments.name: Antifungal prophylaxis (posaconazole or fluconazole) during high-risk periods
medicalTreatments.description: In haematology units, high-risk patients undergoing intensive chemotherapy receive prophylactic antifungals. While primarily targeting Aspergillus and Candida, broad-spectrum prophylaxis with posaconazole theoretically reduces geotrichosis risk too.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prophylaxis reduces overall invasive fungal infection rates by roughly 50% in high-risk haematology patients per Cochrane meta-analyses, with geotrichosis too rare to assess individually.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debridement of necrotic pulmonary or soft-tissue lesions
surgicalOptions.description: In cases where localised geotrichosis produces a cavitary lung lesion or soft-tissue abscess that fails to resolve with antifungal therapy, surgical resection or drainage removes the fungal burden and source of ongoing infection.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Outcome data are limited to case reports, but successful resolution is described in roughly 70-80% of cases where surgery is feasible and the patient's immune status allows wound healing.

alternativeTreatments.name: Restoration of immune function
alternativeTreatments.description: Addressing the root cause of immunosuppression — starting antiretroviral therapy for HIV, tapering steroids, or allowing post-chemotherapy neutrophil recovery — is the most effective 'treatment' for geotrichosis and is always pursued alongside antifungals.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: In malnourished patients, repletion of calories, protein, and micronutrients including zinc and vitamin D supports restoration of innate immune defences and improves antifungal treatment response.

whySeeSpecialist: Geotrichosis requires management by an infectious disease specialist, often working alongside a haematologist or transplant physician. The rarity of the condition means primary care clinicians and even general internists may not recognise it, and antifungal selection requires knowledge of in vitro susceptibility patterns and potential drug interactions with immunosuppressive regimens. A mycology-experienced laboratory is also essential for accurate species identification.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an infectious disease physician with experience managing invasive fungal infections in immunocompromised patients, ideally affiliated with a centre that performs bone marrow transplantation or haematological oncology. Ask whether the centre has access to a clinical mycology laboratory capable of performing antifungal susceptibility testing and MALDI-TOF species identification. For HIV-associated cases, a combined ID and HIV specialist clinic is ideal.
preventionStrategies: Maintain antifungal prophylaxis during high-risk periods such as induction chemotherapy for leukaemia or immediately post-transplant, as directed by the treating haematologist.
Start or optimise antiretroviral therapy promptly in HIV-positive individuals to keep CD4 counts above the threshold where opportunistic infections emerge.
Use the lowest effective dose of corticosteroids and other immunosuppressants, and taper them at the earliest clinically safe opportunity.
Avoid unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic courses that deplete the protective gut microbiota and allow fungal overgrowth.
Immunocompromised individuals should avoid unpasteurised dairy products, rotting vegetation, and soil exposure where feasible to reduce environmental inoculum.
Maintain adequate nutritional status in patients undergoing chemotherapy or other prolonged treatments that deplete immunity.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere strictly to antifungal prophylaxis schedules if prescribed during periods of immunosuppression.
Stay well hydrated and maintain oral hygiene to reduce the risk of oral Geotrichum overgrowth.
Report new respiratory symptoms, white oral patches, or persistent diarrhoea promptly to your clinical team rather than waiting for a scheduled appointment.
Avoid close contact with high-risk environmental sources (soil, mouldy organic matter, raw dairy) during periods of profound immune suppression.
Track your neutrophil counts and understand the 'danger window' periods to remain alert to infection signs.

recommended: High-protein foods to support immune function and wound healing during recovery
Pasteurised dairy products rather than raw or unpasteurised milk and cheese
Zinc and vitamin D-rich foods to support innate immunity
Adequate caloric intake to prevent or correct malnutrition
avoid: Unpasteurised cheeses and dairy products, which can contain viable Geotrichum organisms
Rotting or mouldy fruit, vegetables, and fermented foods that may harbour environmental fungi
Excessive alcohol, which impairs neutrophil and macrophage function

exerciseGuidelines: During active infection or periods of profound neutropenia, strenuous exercise should be avoided as it can transiently impair immune function and carries a fall/injury risk when platelet counts are low. Light walking and gentle movement help maintain muscle mass and mood during prolonged treatment. Once infection is controlled and blood counts recover, a gradual return to normal activity is encouraged.
dailyManagement: Take antifungal medications at the same time each day and avoid missing doses, as subtherapeutic levels allow fungal regrowth.
Monitor for antifungal side effects — itraconazole can cause liver enzyme elevation; report jaundice, dark urine, or severe nausea promptly.
Attend all scheduled blood-test appointments so that antifungal drug levels and liver and kidney function can be monitored.
Inform every prescriber about your antifungal therapy, as azoles interact with many drugs including calcineurin inhibitors used after transplantation.
Seek same-day review if you develop new or worsening fever, difficulty breathing, or rapid clinical deterioration.
prognosis: The outcome of geotrichosis depends far more on the underlying immune status than on the fungal infection itself. Localised bronchopulmonary or gastrointestinal geotrichosis in patients whose immunity can be at least partially restored generally responds well to 4-12 weeks of antifungal therapy, with resolution or significant improvement in the majority of adequately treated cases. Disseminated geotrichosis in patients with refractory or relapsed haematological malignancy or profound sustained neutropenia carries a mortality rate estimated at 40-70% in case series, reflecting the severity of the underlying disease rather than antifungal resistance alone. Successful antiretroviral therapy initiation in HIV-associated cases has been associated with favourable outcomes, mirroring the experience with other opportunistic infections where immune reconstitution is the primary driver of recovery. Long-term pulmonary sequelae such as cavitary scarring are occasionally reported after severe bronchopulmonary disease.
recoveryTimeline: Patients with localised disease and a partially correctable immune defect typically see clinical improvement — reduced fever, decreasing sputum production, or healing of oral lesions — within 2-4 weeks of starting appropriate antifungal therapy. Radiological clearing of pulmonary infiltrates may lag behind clinical improvement by several additional weeks. Antifungal therapy is generally continued for a minimum of 4-8 weeks for localised disease and 2-6 months or until sustained immune recovery for disseminated disease. Full functional recovery after severe illness is measured in months.
complications: Fungaemia and disseminated geotrichosis — bloodstream spread to the liver, spleen, kidneys, or brain; occurring primarily in profoundly neutropenic patients and carrying the highest mortality.
Respiratory failure — extensive pulmonary infiltrates can compromise gas exchange, requiring supplemental oxygen or mechanical ventilation in severe cases.
Persistent pulmonary cavitation and fibrosis — a long-term sequela of severe bronchopulmonary infection that may cause lasting reduction in lung function.
Drug toxicity from prolonged antifungal therapy — itraconazole-related hepatotoxicity and amphotericin B-related nephrotoxicity and electrolyte disturbances require regular monitoring.

Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) — in HIV patients starting antiretroviral therapy, rapid recovery of immunity can paradoxically trigger worsening inflammation at the site of fungal infection, mimicking clinical deterioration.

supportResources.name: CDC — Fungal Diseases
supportResources.description: Overview of opportunistic fungal infections from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, with guidance on at-risk populations.

supportResources.name: NIH AIDSinfo — Guidelines for the Prevention and Treatment of Opportunistic Infections in Adults and Adolescents with HIV
supportResources.description: Comprehensive HIV opportunistic infection guidelines relevant to immunocompromised patients at risk for geotrichosis.

supportResources.name: ECMM — European Confederation of Medical Mycology
supportResources.description: Professional body for medical mycology in Europe, with resources on rare fungal infections and expert centre referrals.

sources.title: De Hoog GS et al. Taxonomy of medically important fungi: Geotrichum and Galactomyces. Studies in Mycology, 2014
Ruan SY et al. Invasive Trichosporon and Geotrichum infections: case series and literature review. BMC Infectious Diseases, 2009
Arendrup MC et al. ESCMID and ECMM Joint Clinical Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Rare Invasive Yeast Infections. Clinical Microbiology and Infection, 2014

confusedWithConditions.name: Oral candidiasis (thrush)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause white oral plaques in immunocompromised patients, but geotrichosis is identified by barrel-shaped arthroconidia rather than Candida pseudohyphae on microscopy, and may respond differently to antifungals.

confusedWithConditions.name: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both affect immunocompromised patients with pulmonary infiltrates, but aspergillosis produces septate hyphae with characteristic dichotomous branching and elevated galactomannan, whereas geotrichosis shows arthroconidia and is galactomannan-negative.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TB causes chronic cough and upper-lobe infiltrates resembling bronchopulmonary geotrichosis, but is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, responds to antibacterials not antifungals, and is confirmed by AFB smear and culture or molecular testing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary candidiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both are opportunistic fungal infections of the lung in immunosuppressed patients; differentiated by organism morphology and culture: Candida forms pseudohyphae while Geotrichum forms chains of arthroconidia.

relatedConditions.name: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Like geotrichosis, IPA is an opportunistic mould infection occurring in severely immunocompromised patients and may require concurrent management in haematology settings.

relatedConditions.name: Disseminated cryptococcosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both geotrichosis and cryptococcosis are rare opportunistic fungal infections that disseminate in HIV/AIDS patients; cryptococcosis is far more common and provides useful comparative diagnostic and treatment context.

relatedConditions.name: Pulmonary mucormycosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Mucormycosis shares the same high-risk immunocompromised host population as geotrichosis and presents with overlapping pulmonary infiltrates, requiring mycological distinction for appropriate antifungal selection.

relatedConditions.name: Disseminated aspergillosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Disseminated aspergillosis and disseminated geotrichosis both occur in profoundly neutropenic haematology patients and share the clinical challenge of fungal spread to multiple organs.

faqs.question: What is geotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Geotrichosis is a rare fungal infection caused by Geotrichum candidum. It mainly affects people with weakened immune systems, such as those with HIV/AIDS, leukaemia, or organ transplants. It can infect the lungs, mouth, gut, or spread through the bloodstream in severe cases.

faqs.question: How do you get geotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Geotrichum candidum lives in soil, dairy products, and the normal human gut. Infection usually occurs when a person's immune defences fail, allowing the organism already present in the body to overgrow, or after inhaling spores from the environment. It is not contagious between people.

faqs.question: Who is most at risk of geotrichosis?
faqs.answer: People with HIV/AIDS and low CD4 counts, patients undergoing chemotherapy for leukaemia or lymphoma, organ transplant recipients on immunosuppression, and those on long-term high-dose corticosteroids are at highest risk. Healthy people with normal immune systems almost never develop invasive geotrichosis.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of geotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Symptoms depend on the affected site. Pulmonary geotrichosis causes chronic cough, bloody sputum, and fever. Oral geotrichosis produces white mouth plaques. Gut involvement causes diarrhoea and abdominal cramps. Disseminated disease causes high fever, chills, and multi-organ involvement.

faqs.question: How is geotrichosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires fungal culture of sputum, stool, or tissue showing Geotrichum candidum, confirmed by microscopy revealing characteristic barrel-shaped arthroconidia. Blood cultures detect bloodstream spread. Chest CT shows pulmonary involvement. Biopsy with histopathology is the definitive test.

faqs.question: What antibiotic treats geotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Geotrichosis is a fungal infection, not bacterial, so antibiotics do not work. Treatment uses antifungal drugs. Itraconazole is first-line for localised disease. Severe or disseminated cases are treated with intravenous liposomal amphotericin B. Voriconazole is an alternative.

faqs.question: Is geotrichosis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Geotrichosis is not transmitted from person to person. It develops from an organism already present in the patient's own body or from environmental exposure to soil or dairy. There is no need to isolate patients with geotrichosis from others.

faqs.question: Can geotrichosis be cured?
faqs.answer: Many patients with localised geotrichosis who receive appropriate antifungal therapy and whose immune function can be restored achieve full resolution of the infection. Disseminated disease in patients with irreversible immune failure has a much poorer outlook, with mortality estimated at 40-70% in reported case series.

faqs.question: How long does treatment for geotrichosis last?
faqs.answer: Localised geotrichosis is typically treated for 4-8 weeks with itraconazole. Disseminated disease requires intravenous amphotericin B for 2-6 weeks or longer, followed by oral maintenance therapy until immune recovery. Duration depends on clinical response and the patient's ability to restore immune function.

faqs.question: Is fluconazole effective against geotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Fluconazole has limited effectiveness against Geotrichum candidum, which shows reduced susceptibility to this azole in laboratory testing. Itraconazole or voriconazole are preferred for azole-based treatment. Antifungal susceptibility testing should guide drug selection whenever an isolate is available.

faqs.question: Can geotrichosis affect the lungs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bronchopulmonary geotrichosis is the most commonly reported clinically significant form. It presents with chronic productive cough, haemoptysis, low-grade fever, and radiological infiltrates that can be mistaken for tuberculosis or aspergillosis. It almost always occurs in immunocompromised patients.

faqs.question: How common is geotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Geotrichosis is very rare; fewer than 200 well-documented cases appear in the modern medical literature. However, Geotrichum itself colonises the gut of up to 30% of healthy adults without causing harm. Invasive infection is uncommon even among immunocompromised patients.

faqs.question: Can geotrichosis spread to the brain?
faqs.answer: CNS involvement is rare but documented in case reports of disseminated geotrichosis, presenting as confusion, focal neurological deficits, or meningitis. It carries a very poor prognosis and occurs almost exclusively in patients with profound and sustained immune failure such as refractory leukaemia.

faqs.question: Does geotrichosis look like oral thrush?
faqs.answer: Yes. Oral geotrichosis produces white patches on the tongue and inner cheeks that closely resemble oral candidiasis (thrush). The key distinction is microscopic: geotrichosis shows barrel-shaped arthroconidia rather than the pseudohyphae of Candida. Culture and microscopy are needed to differentiate them.

faqs.question: What happens if geotrichosis is untreated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment in an immunocompromised patient, geotrichosis can progress from localised infection to bloodstream spread (fungaemia) and multi-organ failure. The pace of progression depends on the degree of immune suppression. Early diagnosis and treatment significantly improve outcomes compared to delayed recognition.

faqs.question: Can healthy people get geotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Invasive geotrichosis is extremely rare in immunocompetent individuals. Geotrichum candidum colonises healthy adults routinely in the gut without causing disease. The few reported cases in otherwise healthy people have involved very high environmental exposure or unrecognised immune defects.

faqs.question: Is geotrichosis related to athlete's foot or ringworm?
faqs.answer: No. Athlete's foot and ringworm are caused by dermatophytes — a completely different group of fungi (Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton). Geotrichum candidum is an ascomycete in a separate fungal family and causes a very different type of infection primarily affecting internal organs in immunocompromised patients.

faqs.question: Can geotrichosis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Relapse is possible, particularly if the underlying immune defect is not corrected. Patients with HIV who achieve immune reconstitution on antiretroviral therapy or cancer patients who achieve remission have much lower relapse rates. Long-term secondary prophylaxis with itraconazole may be continued until immune recovery is confirmed.

faqs.question: What blood tests are used in geotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Blood cultures detect fungaemia in disseminated disease. Serum beta-D-glucan, a broad fungal marker, may be elevated but is non-specific. A full blood count assesses neutrophil counts, liver function tests monitor for antifungal hepatotoxicity, and renal function tests track potential amphotericin B nephrotoxicity.

faqs.question: Should I avoid certain foods if I have geotrichosis or am at risk?
faqs.answer: Immunocompromised patients should avoid unpasteurised dairy products — raw milk and artisan cheeses — that can contain viable Geotrichum organisms. High-protein, calorie-adequate nutrition supports immune recovery. Alcohol impairs neutrophil function and is best minimised during treatment.

metaDescription: Geotrichosis is a rare opportunistic fungal infection causing lung, oral and gut symptoms in immunocompromised patients. Learn causes, diagnosis and treatment.
